entry.content:
64. Minimum Path Sum

  • Method 1

    Use dp, cur[j] stands for the minimum length of reaching the jth column ( in the ith loop ).

    Remember to carefully initialize the situation.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(m*n)

    Solution:

63. Unique Paths II

  • Method 1

    Like in


    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(m*n)

    Solution:

62. Unique Paths

  • Method 1

    Use dp. Init d[m*n] with 1.
    $$dp[i, j] = dp[i-1, j] + dp[i, j-1]$$
    To save memory, we only need d[n], and
    $$ d[n] += d[n-1] $$


    Complexity
    MemoryO(min(m, n))
    TimeO(m*n)

    Solution:

  • Method 2

    This is math solution. We can prove that the number of f(m, n) = $C^{m-1}_{m+n-2}$, and there is also a detailed explanation for it by me.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(1)
    TimeO(min(m, n))

    Solution:

61. Rotate List

  • Method 1

    Calculate the length, find the node to break, change the next of start and end.

    Use a dummy for convenience.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(1)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

60. Permutation Sequence

  • Method 1

    The idea is simple, just choose the first, then the second… The only thing to pay attention to is that we need to use a vector to store numbers 1-n and when one of them is added to the string, we need to erase it.

    I don’t understand why this problem is hard… It is easier than many middle problems.

    In addition, if we want a range of arrays, like the amount of n/2, then we can use the method in 47. Permutations II, which actually gives all the permutations in the correct order.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

58. Length of Last Word

  • Method 1

    Just loop from behind.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(1)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

57. Insert Interval

  • Method 1

    So just loop through the intervals. If there is intersect, we keep track of the changed interval until it will not influence other intervals.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

• Method 1

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

55. Jump Game

  • Method 1

    Use dp to record the maximum array that ends at i.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n)

  • Method 2

    Use the same idea in method 1, but since in the state transfer formula we only need nums[i] and curLen, so we don’t actually need a dp array and can save some memory.

    Funnily, the explanations of this problem is almost the same as problem 53, lol

    Complexity
    MemoryO(1)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

54. Spiral Matrix

  • Method 1

    Boring matrix problem.

    Remember that when width or height < 2, we have to deal with them independently. Because correctness is more important than so-called elegance of code.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(mn)
    TimeO(mn)

    Solution:

53. Maximum Subarray

  • Method 1

    Use dp to record the maximum array that ends at i.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n)

  • Method 2

    Use the same idea in method 1, but since in the state transfer formula we only need nums[i-1] and curLen, so we don’t actually need a dp array and can save some memory.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(1)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

52. N-Queens II

  • Method 1

    Use dfs(backtracking), and 3 hashmaps.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n!)

    Solution:

51. N-Queens

  • Method 1

    Use hashmap and dfs.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n!)

    Solution:

50. Pow(x, n)

  • Method 1

    Recursive quick pow.

    Pay attention to edge cases like INT_MIN.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(1)
    TimeO(logn)

    Solution:

65. Valid Number

  • Method 1

    Just write judge integar and decimal function and do discussion.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(n)
    TimeO(n)

    Solution:

entry.title: Writing trial
entry.content:
44. Wildcard Matching

  • Method 1

    Use dp. The transfer formula is like problem 10 but differs a bit. Written in the comment of code.

    Complexity
    MemoryO(mn)
    TimeO(mn)

    Solution:

entry.content: 这是我的第一篇博客！
